observations.text: Age: Seed states 34; interview confirms age 34 with DOB August 14, 1991 (Q85), and Maria references being thirty-four in Q03 (goal to get GED 'by the time I'm thirty-five' and 'I'm thirty-four now'). Fully consistent.
Gender: Seed states Female; interview consistently presents Maria as a woman, mother, wife throughout all responses. Confirmed explicitly in Q84 and Q88. Fully consistent.
Marital status: Seed states married_present; interview confirms married for twelve years to Carlos, living together with two children (Q05, Q84). Fully consistent.

Education: Seed states 9th_12th_no_diploma; interview specifies Maria dropped out during 11th grade ('my junior year') and has no diploma (Q01, Q90). Currently self-studying for GED (Q91). Consistent and enriched.

Occupation: Seed states no_occupation; interview confirms no formal employment -- Maria describes herself as at-home with children, doing informal catering and volunteer event planning (Q16, Q65, Q76). Consistent.

Location: Seed states Lilburn, GA 30047; interview confirms lifelong residence in Lilburn, Gwinnett County, with detailed neighborhood descriptions (Q01, Q08, Q87, Q97). Born at hospital in Lawrenceville but raised in Lilburn. Fully consistent.

Household composition: Interview describes four-person household (Maria, Carlos 36, Diego 10, Sofia 7) plus dog Canela (Q05, Q11, Q51). Extracted profile captures location and household structure in home_and_lifestyle but does not include a dedicated 'household' section with all members' ages. Partial capture.

Rent amount and trajectory: Interview specifies $1,450/month rent, up from $1,100 five years ago (Q10, Q56, Q58). Profile accurately captures both current rent and the increase trajectory. Consistent.

Parents' education and father's details: Interview provides father's education (~6th-7th grade, Q100), parents' separation (Q05), father's current location ('other side of town,' Q05), and brother Luis's details (age 28, community college, car dealership, Q05). Profile's demographer summary mentions brother and mother but omits father's education level and current situation.
Renting versus owning: Interview confirms renting (Q58), homeownership is a distant dream. Profile captures renting status accurately. Consistent.

PeachCare for children: Interview mentions kids on PeachCare, Georgia's children's health program (Q40, Q80). Profile does not explicitly mention PeachCare as a government benefit in any preference section.

Age and life timeline: Profile states age 34, married 12 years (married at 22), met Carlos at 19. Diego is 10 (born when Maria was ~23, one year after marriage). Sofia is 7 (born when Maria was ~27). These ages and timelines are internally consistent.

Income figures: Profile states $400-$800/month catering income in work_and_career details, while demographer summary says the same range. Household income described as ~$3,200 from Carlos plus Maria's contribution. These are internally consistent across profile sections.

Education status: Profile states '11th grade, no diploma; self-studying for GED' in the education field, and work_and_career preferences mention GED study and Gwinnett Technical College. Others_misc life_events says 'plans to earn GED by age 35.' All internally consistent.

Father's background and current status: Interview provides father's education (~6th-7th grade in Mexico, Q100), occupation (landscaping and construction, Q105-Q107), and current relationship status with Maria (split from mother when Maria was in her twenties, lives across town, 'we talk but it's not like it was,' Q05). None of this appears in the extracted profile.

Brother Luis's details: Interview identifies Luis as 28, finished school, attended community college, works at car dealership (Q05). He also served as a political information source (Q23 follow-up). Not captured in any profile section.
Government benefits: PeachCare enrollment for children, and the fact that the family was over the SNAP income limit by a small margin (Q80), are significant economic details absent from the profile.

Specific debt and savings figures: Interview details $1,800 credit card debt, ~$600 medical debt, $200 owed to mother, $400 savings, $100-$150 emergency cash (Q61, Q63). Profile's behavioral_economist summary references financial stress but does not capture these specific figures.

Budgeting expertise: Seed's professional_persona and skills_and_expertise mention 'methodical budgeting expertise' and 'household budgeting and finance management.' Interview confirms through detailed descriptions of meal planning, coupon use, bulk purchasing, and Sunday budget-planning sessions with Carlos (Q56, Q61, Q83). Consistent and richly elaborated.
observations.source_a_ref: skills_and_expertise: household budgeting and finance management

observations.text: Event planning and financial management: Seed describes event budgeting at an arts center; interview confirms Maria managed a $2,000 budget for Dia de los Muertos, secured five business donations, and tracks all expenses in a notebook (Q02, Q16, Q65). Consistent.

Catering income: Seed's career_goals mentions launching a home-based catering business. Interview confirms Maria is already doing this informally, charging $150-$600 per event, earning $400-$800/month (Q16, Q72). The seed describes an aspiration; the interview reveals it is already partially realized.

Travel and leisure spending: Seed's travel_persona describes weekend road trips to Appalachian foothills, annual Savannah trips, and European getaways to Barcelona and Lisbon. Interview reveals zero discretionary travel beyond local outings and one Six Flags trip using tax refund money (Q75). Budget constraints make seed travel aspirations unrealistic for current economic situation.

Certification pursuit: Seed mentions 'actively pursuing certification in community development.' Interview reveals Maria is self-studying for GED with workbooks and YouTube but has not enrolled in any program (Q18, Q91, Q92). The seed overstates the formality of her educational pursuit.

Loss aversion around savings: Interview describes Maria's distress at depleting the $400 savings buffer, the rule with Carlos to not go below $200, and the feeling of 'going backwards' when withdrawing (Q61). Profile's behavioral_economist summary accurately captures this as 'extreme present-orientation driven by necessity rather than preference' and 'acute psychological distress at the prospect of depleting' savings. Faithfully represented.

Income shock response: Interview details Maria's multi-pronged response to Carlos's reduced hours: selling tamales through Facebook ($10/dozen, ~30 dozen), borrowing $200 from mother, cutting all discretionary spending, cancelling yoga (Q81). Profile's behavioral_economist summary mentions 'immediate behavioral adjustments' including all these strategies. Accurately captured.

Health care trade-offs: Interview reveals Maria skipped her own checkups for two years to fund children's appointments, refused ER visit during 3-day headache due to cost, and owes $600 from Sofia's ear infection (Q36, Q40). Profile accurately characterizes this as 'systematic self-sacrifice pattern in household resource allocation.' Faithfully captured.

Tax refund allocation: Interview describes spending the $2,800 refund on credit card payoff, savings, kids' clothes, porch repair, and Six Flags (Q75). Profile does not capture tax refund behavior or the fact that it functions as the family's primary mechanism for catching up on deferred needs.

Credit card debt and interest awareness: Maria acknowledges paying minimums on ~$1,800 in credit card debt and knowing 'the interest' is a problem (Q63). Profile's behavioral_economist summary does not mention credit card debt or interest burden specifically.

Catering income range: Profile's work_and_career section says $400-$800/month from catering. The behavioral_economist summary references her 'entrepreneurial resourcefulness' and mentions scaling work based on need. In the same month she reports $750 total (including $100 arts center stipend), so ~$650 from catering, which falls within the stated range. Internally consistent.

GED as human capital investment: Profile's behavioral_economist summary calls GED pursuit 'a calculated human capital investment despite high opportunity cost.' Profile's work_and_career preferences describe it as self-study. Profile's others_misc mentions the timeline goal (by age 35). All three references consistently characterize the same effort from different angles.

Specific monthly expense breakdown: Interview provides detailed monthly costs -- $1,450 rent, $320 car payment, ~$250 utilities, $500-$600 groceries, $50 medical payment, plus gas and insurance (Q56). Profile does not capture this granular expense data anywhere.

Seasonal expense fluctuation: Interview describes summer electric bills of $180-$200, Christmas costing extra $300-$400, back-to-school expenses in August (Q59). Profile does not capture seasonal budget variation patterns.

Informal lending networks: Interview reveals bidirectional lending -- borrowed $200 from mother (Q63, Q81), $200 from Lupita for car brakes (Q64), and describes this as a normal coping mechanism. Profile mentions 'family lending networks' in behavioral_economist summary but does not capture specific amounts or reciprocity patterns.

Carlos's income variability: Interview details Carlos earning ~$3,200/month after taxes but hours being subject to weather delays and construction cycles (Q38, Q74, Q81). Profile mentions husband's wages but not the variability or its impact on household economic stability.

Community organizing: Seed's professional_persona describes Maria designing 'inclusive festivals, workshops, and cultural programs for a local arts center.' Interview confirms extensive event planning: Dia de los Muertos festival (Q02), spring art walk (Q16, Q71), neighborhood potlucks (Q08), church festivals (Q47). Consistent and deeply elaborated.

Civic engagement: Seed mentions no political or civic engagement. Interview reveals Maria is a registered voter (Q23), attends county commission meetings (Q25), and discusses race and policing issues (Q26-Q30). Interview significantly enriches the civic dimension absent from seed.

Social media community moderation: Seed lists 'social media community moderation' as a skill. Interview confirms Maria moderates three Facebook groups (Lilburn neighborhood, Mexican families in Gwinnett, arts center events) and actively connects community members with resources (Q48, Q49). Consistent and enriched.

Cross-cultural bridge-building: Seed's cultural_background mentions 'fostering cross-cultural exchange.' Interview confirms multicultural potlucks (Korean BBQ next to tamales, Q08), diverse book club membership, and inclusive event planning philosophy (Q29). Consistent.

Church as community hub: Seed mentions 'Catholic upbringing influences her participation in church activities, which serve as a hub for both spiritual and social connections.' Interview confirms extensive church involvement -- volunteering, event planning, youth programs, Las Posadas, Dia de los Muertos altars, and Father Morales as trusted community figure (Q47). Fully consistent.

Voting behavior: Interview specifies Maria registered to vote at 26-27, votes in presidential elections, misses smaller ones (Q23). Profile's political_scientist summary accurately describes her as participating in presidential elections 'but inconsistently in lower-ballot races, reflecting an information-cost barrier rather than apathy.' Faithfully represented.

County commission attendance: Interview describes Maria attending 4-5 county commission meetings after being introduced by Janet from book club (Q25). Profile's community_and_civic preferences and political_scientist summary both capture this. Consistent.

Political identity: Interview reveals Maria is issue-driven rather than party-identified, leans toward the party that supports working families, health care, and schools, but holds traditional family values on social issues (Q24, Q95, Q96). Profile's political_scientist summary accurately describes this as 'economically progressive, culturally traditional, institutionally pragmatic.' Faithfully captured.

Response to racial justice: Interview details Maria's empathetic but cautious stance -- discusses issues privately, shares Facebook posts, builds inclusive events rather than protesting (Q26, Q27, Q29). Profile's political_scientist summary describes this as 'behaviorally cautious -- she discusses issues privately and promotes inclusion through her event work rather than engaging in protest.' Accurate.

Immigration-related anxiety: Interview reveals background awareness about documentation and policing for Mexican families (Q20, Q26 follow-up), including neighbors who avoid calling police due to status fears. Profile mentions 'fair treatment of immigrant-origin families' as a political issue but does not fully capture the day-to-day dimension of this awareness.

Civic engagement characterization: Profile's community_and_civic preferences list 'recently started attending Gwinnett County commission meetings' and 'registered voter; votes in presidential and some local elections.' The political_scientist summary describes an 'engagement trajectory moving from disengagement to increasing participation.' These are consistent -- the preferences show the behavior, the summary explains the trend.

Facebook community management as civic infrastructure: Profile's technology preferences describe managing three Facebook groups. Profile's political_scientist summary characterizes this as 'informal civic infrastructure.' These assessments are compatible and complementary.

Cultural traditionalism and social progressivism: Profile's political_scientist summary says 'economically progressive, culturally traditional,' which is consistent with the psychologist summary's mention of faith and family values. Both summaries acknowledge the same tension without contradicting each other.

Carlos's political stance: Interview describes Carlos as politically disengaged ('just let me work and take care of my family') but occasionally fired up about construction worker or immigration issues (Q24 follow-up). This household political dynamic is absent from the profile.

Generational divide on BLM in Mexican community: Interview describes older generation's reluctance to engage ('that's their business') versus younger generation's vocal support, with Maria positioning herself in between (Q27, Q30). This intracommunity political dynamic is not captured in the profile.

Neighbor's college-age daughter as political influence: Interview identifies this young woman as someone who taught Maria about solidarity and intersectionality (Q30 follow-up). This cross-generational political learning relationship is absent from the profile.

Caretaking pattern: Seed's persona describes Maria channeling 'curiosity and methodical energy into weaving art, food, and community together.' Interview reveals a deeply embedded caretaker pattern -- caring for brother as a teenager, mother as an adult, children, church community, neighborhood. Maria's identity is structured around being needed (Q01, Q05, Q17, Q35, Q44, Q109). Consistent with seed's community-focused framing but significantly deeper.

Quirky detail -- hoarding kitchen gadgets: Seed mentions Maria 'occasionally hoards seldom-used kitchen gadgets.' This specific behavioral detail does not appear anywhere in the interview. It was neither confirmed nor contradicted.

Forgetting to water garden: Seed mentions Maria 'forgets to water their garden during particularly busy weeks.' Interview confirms this behavior -- 'Sometimes I forget to water everything when I'm really busy with event planning and stuff comes back kind of sad looking' (Q17 follow-up). Consistent.

Artistic engagement: Seed's arts_persona mentions watercolor, pottery, folk dance, artists (O'Keeffe, Monet, Pollock), and a multicultural book club with specific authors (Morrison, Murakami, Adichie). Interview confirms folk dance (Q01, Q15), book club with Morrison and Adichie (Q07), and watercolor/pottery at the School of the Arts (seed reference, not directly in interview). The specific artist names (O'Keeffe, Monet, Pollock) and Murakami as book club author do not surface in the interview.

Coping through cooking: Seed mentions Maria 'channels curiosity and methodical energy' through food and community. Interview vividly confirms cooking as emotional regulation -- making 60 tamales at midnight during financial stress (Q38), the kitchen as 'therapy' (Q38). Behavioral pattern is consistent with seed's description and deeply elaborated.

Co-ed recreational soccer league: Seed's sports_persona states Maria 'participates in a co-ed recreational soccer league each spring.' Interview never mentions Maria playing soccer herself -- only son Diego plays soccer (Q05, Q19). This may be a seed element that does not represent current behavior.
observations.source_b_ref: Q05, Q15, Q19 (only Diego's soccer mentioned)

observations.text: Emotional breakdown episode: Interview provides a detailed account of the fall 2025 crisis -- sitting on the bathroom floor crying, feeling overwhelmed by converging financial, parenting, and caregiving demands (Q44). Profile's psychologist summary accurately captures this as revealing 'her emotional regulation system can be overwhelmed when multiple role demands converge simultaneously.' Faithfully represented.

Nighttime rumination: Interview describes lying awake running through financial worries, children's futures, and mother's health (Q43). Profile's others_misc captures 'poor sleep quality: lies awake ruminating about finances and family concerns' and psychologist summary mentions 'Neuroticism manifests as nighttime rumination.' Accurately represented in both sections.

Coping mechanisms inventory: Interview identifies cooking (Q38), yoga breathing (Q45 follow-up), garden time (Q43 follow-up), Lupita check-ins (Q44 follow-up), and Mass attendance (Q44 follow-up) as coping strategies. Profile's psychologist summary mentions 'cooking as emotional regulation, yoga breathing, social support from Lupita.' Garden time and Mass are mentioned in preferences but not explicitly in the psychologist's coping framework.
observations.source_a_ref: Q38, Q43 follow-up, Q44 follow-up, Q45 follow-up

observations.text: Self-concept evolution: Interview traces a shift from 'just a helper' to recognizing herself as a leader after the Dia de los Muertos festival (Q02 follow-up). Profile's psychologist summary captures the 'tension between her caretaker identity (always helping others) and her emerging recognition that she must invest in herself (GED, career).' Faithfully represented.

Children's behavioral responses to household stress: Interview describes Diego becoming withdrawn and drawing for hours, Sofia becoming clingy (Q46). This family stress-response pattern is absent from the profile entirely.

Conscientiousness characterization: Psychologist summary describes 'high Conscientiousness through meticulous event budgeting, meal planning, and household management.' Behavioral_economist summary describes the same meticulous budgeting and planning. These cross-expert characterizations are consistent.

Openness to Experience: Psychologist summary states 'Openness to Experience is notable in her artistic pursuits (dance, watercolor, pottery, multicultural reading) and willingness to experiment with fusion cooking, though constrained by economic realities.' This is consistent with the hobbies_and_recreation and arts_and_culture preference sections which list all these activities. No contradiction.

Relational style: Psychologist summary says 'clear attachment to a small number of deep relationships (Lupita, mother, Ana) rather than broad shallow networks.' Social_and_relationships preferences list the same core relationships. However, the preference section also lists broader ties (book club, church community, Facebook groups), which the psychologist correctly distinguishes from the deep attachment pattern. Internally consistent.

Guilt pattern: Interview reveals Maria feels guilt when unable to provide (shoes for Diego, Q64 follow-up), guilt when others cover for her during illness (Q33 follow-up), and guilt about 'dropping the ball' as a mother (Q43). This recurrent guilt pattern is not explicitly named in the psychologist summary.

Cultural barriers to help-seeking: Interview describes Maria's awareness that her culture discourages professional help ('you're supposed to be strong, pray about it, keep going,' Q45) but her own attitude is shifting. She identifies practical barriers (cost, time, Spanish-speaking availability) over stigma. This nuanced shift in help-seeking attitudes is captured only briefly ('I'm starting to think differently about that') and not fully in the profile.

Parenting motivation as primary driver: Interview repeatedly frames Maria's decisions through the lens of her children's future -- staying in the neighborhood for schools, pursuing GED so children see her succeed, wanting children to attend college (Q01, Q43, Q62, Q108, Q109). Profile captures this in multiple places but the psychologist summary could more explicitly foreground motherhood as the central motivational axis.

Diego's bullying experience and Maria's response: Interview reveals Diego was being made fun of at school, came home crying; Maria talked to his teacher, explaining racial jokes ('taco comments,' Q28). This parenting challenge and Maria's protective advocacy response are not captured in the profile.

inconsistencies_found.description: Seed's travel_persona describes active travel (Appalachian road trips, Savannah, Barcelona, Lisbon) that is financially implausible given interview-revealed household economics. The profile acknowledges this gap in unresolved_or_ambiguous but the travel preference section still references these seed destinations.

Seed's professional_persona states Maria is 'actively pursuing certification in community development,' but interview reveals she has not enrolled in any program and is self-studying for GED only (Q91, Q92).

Seed's sports_persona states Maria participates in a co-ed recreational soccer league each spring. Interview contains no mention of Maria playing soccer -- only her son Diego plays. This seed element appears to be unsubstantiated.

resolutions.resolution: Travel preferences should be clearly marked as aspirational seed data that does not reflect current economic reality. The profile already notes this in unresolved_or_ambiguous, which is appropriate.

The interview establishes that the GED is the current active educational pursuit; community development certification is a future goal contingent on GED completion. Profile's work_and_career section correctly reflects this sequencing.

The unresolved_or_ambiguous section already flags this ('Personal soccer participation not mentioned in interview; only son Diego's soccer league discussed. May be aspirational in seed data or a past activity'). This is an appropriate resolution.

gaps_addressed: Father's background, education, and current relationship with Maria should be added to the profile for demographic completeness.
Brother Luis's demographic details (age 28, community college, car dealership) and role as political information source should be captured.
PeachCare enrollment and SNAP ineligibility (marginally over income threshold) should be noted in a financial or government benefits section.
Specific debt figures ($1,800 credit card, $600 medical, $200 to mother) and savings ($400 + $100-$150 cash) should be captured.
Monthly expense breakdown ($1,450 rent, $320 car, ~$250 utilities, $500-$600 groceries) should be noted.
Children's behavioral stress responses (Diego withdrawing, Sofia clinging) should be added to the psychologist domain.
Maria's guilt pattern and cultural barriers to professional help-seeking should be more explicitly addressed.

expert_summaries.demographer: Maria Buendia is a 34-year-old US-born Mexican-American woman living in Lilburn, Georgia (Gwinnett County), where she has resided her entire life (Q01, Q87, Q97). She is married to Carlos, age 36, a construction worker, and they have two children -- Diego (10) and Sofia (7) -- in a four-person household renting a three-bedroom home for $1,450/month (Q05, Q11, Q56, Q58). Her education stopped at 11th grade with no diploma (Q01, Q90), and she has no formal occupation, relying on informal catering income of $400-$800/month to supplement Carlos's construction wages of approximately $3,200/month (Q16, Q72, Q74). The household income places them near the lower-middle-income threshold for a family of four in the Atlanta metro area. Her mother Rosa, age 62, lives 15 minutes away with significant knee problems requiring Maria's caregiving support 2-3 times weekly (Q05, Q35). Her father lives across town following a separation, and her brother Luis, 28, completed community college and works at a car dealership (Q05).
expert_summaries.behavioral_economist: Maria's economic behavior reflects constrained optimization under persistent scarcity rather than preference-driven choices (Q56, Q61, Q83). She demonstrates strong loss aversion around her small savings buffer of approximately $400, describing acute distress when forced to deplete it (Q61, Q82). Her informal catering business shows entrepreneurial resourcefulness: she scales labor supply based on need (selling tamales through Facebook at $10/dozen during income shocks, Q81), uses social networks for client acquisition, and meticulously manages food costs through bulk purchasing, garden cultivation, and strategic shopping at ethnic markets on Buford Highway (Q56). Her decision to forgo personal health care for two or more years while maintaining children's PeachCare coverage reveals systematic self-sacrifice in household resource allocation (Q36, Q40). She responds to income shocks with immediate multi-pronged behavioral adjustments including increased informal labor, discretionary spending cuts, and activation of family lending networks with her mother and Lupita (Q60, Q64, Q81). Her GED pursuit represents a calculated human capital investment despite high opportunity cost from caregiving and household demands (Q18, Q108).
expert_summaries.political_scientist: Maria exhibits a civic engagement trajectory moving from youthful disengagement to increasing participation, catalyzed by parental stakes in public institutions (Q23, Q25). She registered to vote at 26-27 and participates in presidential elections but inconsistently in lower-ballot races, reflecting information-cost barriers rather than apathy (Q23). Her political orientation is issue-driven rather than party-identified, clustering around affordable health care, adequately funded schools, cost of living, and fair treatment of immigrant-origin families (Q24, Q95, Q96). She has recently expanded her civic repertoire to include attending county commission meetings, prompted by social network influence from book club member Janet (Q25). Her community organizing -- moderating three Facebook groups, planning multicultural events, bridging ethnic groups through neighborhood potlucks (Q08, Q48) -- functions as informal civic infrastructure even though she does not frame it politically. Her response to racial justice movements is empathetic and solidarity-oriented but behaviorally cautious, promoting inclusion through event work rather than protest attendance (Q26, Q27, Q29).
expert_summaries.psychologist: Maria displays high conscientiousness through meticulous event budgeting, meal planning, and household management, paired with high agreeableness evident in her relentless caregiving across family and community spheres (Q02, Q15, Q56, Q65). Her emotional world is organized around a caretaker identity that extends from childhood (caring for her brother after her mother worked two jobs, Q01) through adulthood (caring for her mother, children, church community, and neighborhood). A tension exists between this caretaker orientation and her emerging recognition that she must invest in herself, visible in her GED pursuit and career aspirations (Q02 follow-up, Q108). When multiple role demands converge simultaneously -- as during the fall 2025 crisis combining financial stress, Diego's school difficulties, and her mother's health episode -- her emotional regulation system can become overwhelmed, as evidenced by the bathroom-floor crying episode (Q44). She has since developed functional coping mechanisms including cooking as emotional regulation (Q38), yoga breathing techniques (Q45 follow-up), weekly honest check-ins with Lupita (Q44 follow-up), and garden time as a solitary restorative activity (Q43 follow-up). Her relational style is warm and reciprocal, with deep attachments to a small network (Lupita, her mother, Ana from dance) rather than broad shallow connections (Q07, Q05).

additions: Add father's details: separated from mother, lives across town in Lilburn area, ~6th-7th grade education from Mexico, former landscaper and construction worker
Add brother Luis's details: age 28, community college graduate, works at car dealership, serves as political information source
Add PeachCare enrollment for children and SNAP ineligibility (marginally over income limit) to a benefits/government assistance section
Add specific financial figures: $1,800 credit card debt, $600 medical debt (Sofia's ear infection), $200 owed to mother, $400 savings, $100-$150 emergency cash
Add monthly expense breakdown: $1,450 rent, $320 car payment, ~$250 utilities, $500-$600 groceries, $50 medical payment
Add children's stress response patterns: Diego withdraws and draws for hours, Sofia becomes clingy
Add Diego's school bullying experience (racial/ethnic taunting about Mexican heritage) and Maria's advocacy response
corrections: Travel preferences section should explicitly state that Appalachian, Savannah, and European travel references come from seed data only and were not supported by interview content
Sports preferences should note that Maria's personal soccer league participation (from seed) was not confirmed in the interview

final_status: PASS_WITH_REVISIONS. The interview is richly consistent with seed data across all four expert domains. Three minor inconsistencies were identified between seed aspirational content and interview-revealed economic reality (travel, certification status, soccer). The extracted_profile faithfully represents the interview across most domains but has completeness gaps in financial details, father/brother demographics, government benefits, and several behavioral patterns (guilt, children's stress responses, help-seeking attitudes). These are addressable through profile amendments without requiring re-interview.
